content:
Consider a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system, $\boldsymbol{S}$, where

$$ y[n]=S\{x(\mathrm{n})\} $$

$$Let\,\,\,\, S\{\delta[n]\}=\left\{\begin{array}{lc} 1, & n \in\{0,1,2\} \\ 0, & \text { otherwise } \end{array}\right. $$

where $\delta[n]$ is the discrete-time unit impulse function. For an input signal $x[n]$, the output $y[n]$ is
explanation:
Given that the system is LTI and for $i / p \delta(n) \xrightarrow{\text { system }} \mathrm{O} / \mathrm{P}=\{1,1,1\}$ i.e.,

$$ \begin{aligned} h(n) & =\text { impulse response of system } \\ & =\delta(n)+\delta(n-1)+\delta(n-2) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}As\, we \,know,\,\,\, y(n) & =x(n) * h(n) \\ & =x(n) *[\delta(n)+\delta(n-1)+\delta(n-2)] \\ & =x(n)+x(n-1)+x(n-2) \end{aligned} $$

content: Suppose signal $y(t)$ is obtained by the time-reversal of signal $x(t)$, i.e., $y(t) = x(-t)$, $-\infty < t < \infty$. Which one of the following options is always true for the convolution of $x(t)$ and $y(t)$?
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

options.content: If an LTI system is causal, it is stable
A discrete time LTI system is causal if and only if its response to a step input u[n] is 0 for n < 0
If a discrete time LTI system has an impulse response h[n] of finite duration the system is stable
If the impulse response $$0 < |h[n]| < 1$$ for all n, then the LTI system is stable.

explanation:
1. No information about amplitude of $h(n)$ is given. So, option (C) is wrong.

  2. $h(n)$ can have any amplitude less than infinity for the LTI system to be stable. So, option (D) is wrong.

  3. A causal LTI system can also be unstable. So, option (A) is wrong.

  4. If the response to a step input $u[n]$ is 0 for $n<0$, then the discrete time LTI system will be causal. So, option (B) is true.

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: For the signals $$x(t)$$ and $$y(t)$$ shown in the figure, $$z(t)=x(t)*y(t)$$ is maximum at $$t=T_1$$. Then $$T_1$$ in seconds is __________ (Round off to the nearest integer)
explanation:
Given signal y(t) and x(t) is shown in figure below,

Given : $z(t)=x(t) * y(t)=\int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} y(\tau) x(t-\tau) d \tau$

$x(t-\tau)$ can be plotted as shown below,

Maximum value of convolution will be obtained when positive portion of $y(\tau)$ is multiplied with $x(t-\tau)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore 1+t=5 \\\\ & t=4 \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

At $t=4 \mathrm{sec}$, maximum value of convolution will be obtained.

content: If the input $x(t)$ and output $y(t)$ of a system are related as $y(t)=\max [0, x(t)]$, then the system is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given input output relationship is } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & y(t)=\operatorname{Max}[0, x(t)] \\ & y(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 0, & x(t) \leq 0 \\ x(t), & x(t)>0 \end{array}\right. \end{aligned} $$

As output is splitted not in time but for values of $x(t)$, hence the system will not follow superposition, so it is not a linear system.

As there is no time scaling or any coefficient multiplied that is function A time and no extra terms other than $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, so the system is time invariant.

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: Two discrete-time linear time-invariant systems with impulse responses $h_1\lfloor n\rfloor=\delta\lfloor n-1\rfloor+\delta\lfloor n+1\rfloor$ and $h_2[n]=\delta[n]+\delta[n-1]$ are connected in cascade. Where $\delta[n]$ in the Kronecker delta. The impulse response of the cascade system
explanation:
Given : The two systems being cascaded are having impulse responses

$$ \begin{aligned} & h_1[n]=\delta[n-1]+\delta[n+1] \\ & h_2[n]=\delta[n]+\delta[n+1] \end{aligned} $$

Impulse response of the cascaded equivalent system is given as

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & h[n]=h_1[n] * h_2[n]=\{\delta[n-1]+\delta[n+1]\} *\{\delta[n]+\delta[n-1]\} \\ & h[n]=\delta[n-2]+\delta[n-1]+\delta[n]+\delta[n+1] \\ & h[n]=\delta[n-1]+\delta[n]+\delta[n-1]+\delta[n-2] \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Hence, the correct option is (C). } \end{aligned} $$

content:
The impulse response of a causal linear time-invariant system is given as $$h(t)$$. Now consider the following two statements:

Statement-$$\left( {\rm I} \right)$$: Principle of superposition holds
Statement-$$\left( {\rm II} \right)$$: $$h\left( t \right) = 0$$ for $$t < 0$$

Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.content: Statement $$\left( {\rm I} \right)$$ is correct and Statement $$\left( {\rm II} \right)$$ is wrong
Statement $$\left( {\rm II} \right)$$ is correct and Statement $$\left( {\rm I} \right)$$ is wrong
Both Statement $$\left( {\rm I} \right)$$ and Statement $$\left( {\rm II} \right)$$ are wrong
Both Statement $$\left( {\rm I} \right)$$ and Statement $$\left( {\rm II} \right)$$ are correct

content: Let a signal $${a_1}\,\sin \left( {{\omega _1}t + {\phi _1}} \right)$$  be applied to a stable linear time-invariant system. Let the corresponding steady state output be represented as $${a_2}F\left( {{\omega _2}t + {\phi _2}} \right).$$ Then which of the following statements is true?

options.content: $$F$$ is not necessarily a ''sine'' or ''cosine'' function but must be periodic with $${\omega _1} = {\omega _2}.$$
$$F$$ must be a ''sine'' or ''cosine'' function with $${a_1} = {a_2}.$$
$$F$$ must be a ''sine'' function with $${\omega _1} = {\omega _2}.$$  and $${\phi _1} = {\phi _2}.$$
$$F$$ must be a ''sine'' or ''cosine'' function with $${\omega _1} = {\omega _2}.$$

content: $$s(t)$$ is step response and $$h(t)$$ is impulse response of a system. Its response $$y(t)$$ for any input $$u(t)$$ is given by
If $$f(t)$$ is the step-response of a linear time-invariant system, then its impulse response is given by ___________
$$s(t)$$ is step response and $$h(t)$$ is impulse response of a system. Its response $$y(t)$$ for any input $$u(t)$$ is given by

The continuous-time unit impulse signal is applied as an input to a continuous-time linear time-invariant system $S$. The output is observed to be the continuous-time unit step signal $u(t)$. Which one of the following statements is true?

options.content: Every bounded input signal applied to $S$ results in a bounded output signal.
It is possible to find a bounded input signal which when applied to $S$ results in an unbounded output signal.
On applying any input signal to $S$, the output signal is always bounded.
On applying any input signal to $S$ the output signal is always unbounded.

explanation:
In this scenario, the continuous-time unit impulse signal is applied as an input to the continuous-time linear time-invariant system $S$, and the resulting output is the continuous-time unit step signal $ u(t) $.

Given:

The impulse response of the system is $ h(t) = u(t) $.

The input-output relationship for the system is described by:

$ y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau $

Analysis of Options:

Option A: Claims that every bounded input signal applied to $ S $ results in a bounded output signal.

Counterexample: When a step input $ u(t) $ is applied, the output is a ramp signal $ r(t) $. The output for a step input, calculated as:

$ y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau) \, d\tau = r(t) $

This ramp output is unbounded, hence not all bounded inputs produce bounded outputs. Therefore, Option A is incorrect.

Option B: States that it's possible to find a bounded input which, when applied to $ S $, results in an unbounded output.

Example: A step input $ u(t) $ is bounded, but as previously shown, it yields an unbounded ramp output $ r(t) $.

Therefore, Option B is correct since a bounded input (the step) produces an unbounded output.

Option C: Suggests that for any input signal applied to $ S $, the output is always bounded.

Again, the step input example contradicts this, as it produces a ramp, which is unbounded. Thus, Option C is incorrect.

Option D: Proposes that applying any input signal to $ S $ always results in an unbounded output.

Counterexample: If a finite-duration rectangular pulse is used as input, the output remains bounded.

Thus, not all inputs lead to unbounded outputs, making Option D incorrect.

Hence, the correct statement is encapsulated in Option B: a bounded input can indeed result in an unbounded output in this system.

Consider the discrete-time systems $T_1$ and $T_2$ defined as follows:

{ $T_1 x[ n ] = x[ 0 ] + x[ 1 ] + \cdots + x[ n ] $}

{ $T_2 x[ n ] = x[ 0 ] + \frac{1}{2} x[ 1 ] + \cdots + \frac{1}{2^n} x[ n ] $}

Which one of the following statements is true?

options.content: $T_1$ is BIBO stable but $T_2$ is not BIBO stable.
$T_1$ is not BIBO stable but $T_2$ is BIBO stable.

content: Let a causal LTI system be governed by the following differential equation $$y(t) + {1 \over 4}{{dy} \over {dt}} = 2x(t)$$, where x(t) and y(t) are the input and output respectively. Its impulse response is
explanation:
By applying Laplace-transform on given differential equation,

$$y(s) + {1 \over 4}s\,.Y(s) = 2X(s)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{Y(s)} \over {X(s)}} = {2 \over {1 + {s \over 4}}} = {8 \over {s + 4}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow H(s) = {8 \over {s + 4}}$$

By taking inverse LT,

$$h(t) = 8{e^{ - 4t}}u(t)$$

= Impulse response of system.
